perturbation.original_text: In that case, or at any time upon the request of a Provider for any reason, a Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to, within five business days of receipt of such notice, destroy or return all Evaluation Material, except for that portion of notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents or records prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives which does not contain in any respect any of the Evaluation Materials, in any way relating to the Provider or its products, services, employees or other assets or liabilities, and no copy or extract thereof (including electronic copies) shall be retained, except that Recipient’s outside legal counsel may retain one copy to be kept confidential and used solely for the purpose of establishing Recipient’s compliance with its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: In that case, or at any time upon the request of a Provider for any reason, a Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to, within ninety business days of receipt of such notice, destroy or return all Evaluation Material, except for that portion of notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents or records prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives which does not contain in any respect any of the Evaluation Materials, in any way relating to the Provider or its products, services, employees or other assets or liabilities, and no copy or extract thereof (including electronic copies) shall be retained, except that Recipient’s outside legal counsel may retain one copy to be kept confidential and used solely for the purpose of establishing Recipient’s compliance with its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return/destruction deadline to 90 business days is an unreasonably long period for returning confidential information and creates potential risk for the disclosing party. It is also in conflict with general standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act (N.Y. General Business Law § 350-d)
perturbation.law_explanation: Although NY does not have a specific timeframe for the return of confidential information, the 90 business days may constitute a violation of the NY Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as such a long period of possession increases the risk of misappropriation and disclosure of trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The change from 5 business days to 90 business days to return confidential material creates a significant inconsistency with standard practices and increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation. Although the provided scraped law snippet doesn't directly address timeframes for returning confidential information, the Law Explanation states that the extended period may violate the NY Uniform Trade Secrets Act because it increases the risk of misappropriation and disclosure of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Neither Recipient will, within one year from the date of this Agreement, directly or indirectly solicit the employment or consulting services of or employ or engage as a consultant any of the officers or employees of the Provider, so long as they are employed by the Provider and for three months after they cease to be employed by Provider.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Recipient will, within five years from the date of this Agreement, directly or indirectly solicit the employment or consulting services of or employ or engage as a consultant any of the officers or employees of the Provider, so long as they are employed by the Provider and for three months after they cease to be employed by Provider.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the no-solicitation period to five years may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law, especially in the absence of narrowly tailored restrictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A five-year no-solicitation clause may be deemed an unlawful restraint, particularly if it is not narrowly tailored to protect legitimate business interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the no-solicitation period from one year to five years, which the Perturbation and Law explanations suggest may be an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While a one-year restriction might be seen as reasonable, a five-year restriction is more likely to be viewed as an unlawful restraint, thus contradicting this law. The snippet also reinforces this point in 16600(b)(1), stating this section should be read broadly to void noncompete agreements. The change creates a potential legal contradiction because the extended no-solicitation period could be deemed an unlawful restraint on trade, rendering that part of the contract void.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that, for a period of six months from the date of this Agreement (the “ Standstill Period ”), unless specifically invited in writing by the other Party, neither it nor any of its affiliates (as defined in the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the “ Exchange Act ”)) or Representatives (acting in any capacity other than as an advisor in any of the following cases) will in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in, or in any way assist any other Person to effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in: (i) any acquisition of any securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or assets of the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of the other Party; (b) form, join or in any way participate in a “group” (as defined under the Exchange Act) with respect to the securities of the other Party; (c) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit an unsolicited proposal for or offer of (with or without condition), any extraordinary transaction involving the other Party or its securities or assets; (d) otherwise act, alone or in concert with others, to seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or policies of the other Party; (e) take any action which might force the other Party to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in (a) above; or (f) enter into any discussions or arrangements with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that, for a period of ten years from the date of this Agreement (the “ Standstill Period ”), unless specifically invited in writing by the other Party, neither it nor any of its affiliates (as defined in the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the “ Exchange Act ”)) or Representatives (acting in any capacity other than as an advisor in any of the following cases) will in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in, or in any way assist any other Person to effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in: (i) any acquisition of any securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or assets of the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of the other Party; (b) form, join or in any way participate in a “group” (as defined under the Exchange Act) with respect to the securities of the other Party; (c) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit an unsolicited proposal for or offer of (with or without condition), any extraordinary transaction involving the other Party or its securities or assets; (d) otherwise act, alone or in concert with others, to seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or policies of the other Party; (e) take any action which might force the other Party to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in (a) above; or (f) enter into any discussions or arrangements with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: A ten-year standstill provision could be considered an unreasonable restraint on trade or an impermissible restriction on corporate governance rights, potentially conflicting with fiduciary duties under Delaware law if either company is incorporated in Delaware.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law, specifically regarding fiduciary duties
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly prohibiting standstill agreements, Delaware law emphasizes the fiduciary duties of directors to act in the best interests of the corporation. A ten-year standstill, particularly if it prevents directors from considering potentially beneficial transactions for an extended period, might be challenged as a breach of those duties under 8 Del. C. § 141.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The change from a six-month to a ten-year standstill period introduces a significant inconsistency regarding the fiduciary duties of directors. The law explanation and perturbation explanation both suggest that a ten-year standstill could be challenged as a breach of fiduciary duties, particularly if it prevents directors from considering potentially beneficial transactions. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Delaware Code Title 8 Â§ 141, states that "the business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors". A ten-year standstill may unduly restrict the board's ability to manage the corporation effectively, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall terminate two years after the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall terminate one year after the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the agreement's term to one year could be problematic because if trade secrets are expected to maintain their value for a longer time, a one-year protection may be considered insufficient under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.law_explanation: While the DTSA does not specify a minimum term for confidentiality agreements, reducing the term to one year could be seen as insufficient to adequately protect trade secrets, especially if the information's value extends beyond that period. This insufficient protection might be used as evidence against reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, a key element in claiming trade secret misappropriation under the DTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the agreement's term from two years to one year, potentially contradicting the reasonable measures needed to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). While scraped snippet 2 is a dead link, snippet 1 discusses civil actions related to trade secret misappropriation. The law explanation suggests that a shorter term may not adequately protect trade secrets, which could weaken a claim of misappropriation by implying insufficient protection measures. This insufficient protection directly relates to the requirements under 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings, where the applicant needs to show that the information is a trade secret and that reasonable measures were taken to protect it. Reducing the protection period to a single year weakens the 'reasonable measures' argument, creating a contradiction with the intent of the DTSA.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each Company and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to agreements made and to be performed entirely within such State.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each Company and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding its conflict of laws principles, even if all aspects of the agreement are performed in New York.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California while stipulating that all activities happen in New York introduces potential conflicts, especially if California law is less favorable to the agreement's enforcement. This choice of law may be unenforceable under New York conflict of law principles.
perturbation.law_citation: New York choice-of-law principles (e.g., *Schultz v. Boy Scouts of Am., Inc.*, 65 N.Y.2d 189, 491 N.Y.S.2d 90, 480 N.E.2d 686 (1985))
perturbation.law_explanation: New York courts generally respect parties' choice of law, but they apply a 'center of gravity' or 'grouping of contacts' approach when the chosen law has no reasonable relationship to the transaction. Choosing California law when the agreement is made and performed entirely in New York, with parties primarily based in Georgia and Maryland, might render the choice of law clause unenforceable under New York law, causing the court to apply New York law instead.
perturbation.justification: The change introduces a legal contradiction because it alters the governing law of the agreement from New York to California while maintaining that all aspects of the agreement are performed in New York. The Law Explanation states that New York courts apply a 'center of gravity' approach and may disregard the chosen law if it lacks a reasonable relationship to the transaction. The Perturbation Explanation highlights potential conflicts and unenforceability of the California law selection. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition states that a contradiction happens when a contract sets a requirement that violates federal or state law. Therefore, selecting California law when all activities occur in New York creates a conflict that may violate New York's conflict of law principles, rendering the choice of law clause potentially unenforceable.
